title: Kubernetes Deployment Guide
body:
Kubernetes Deployment Guide

This guide covers how to deploy services to Acme Engineering's Kubernetes clusters. All production deployments go through the GitOps pipeline, but manual steps are described here for emergency use and for understanding what the automation does.

Prerequisites

Before deploying, ensure you have the following tools installed and configured:

  • kubectl version 1.28 or later, configured with cluster credentials
  • helm version 3.12 or later
  • Access to the internal Docker registry at registry.acme.internal
  • Your kubeconfig merged for the target cluster (staging or production)

Run kubectl config get-contexts to verify you are pointing at the right cluster. Switch context with kubectl config use-context acme-prod for production or acme-staging for staging.

Deploying a Service

All services at Acme use Helm charts stored in the infra/helm monorepo directory. The standard deploy command is:

helm upgrade --install <service-name> ./infra/helm/<service-name> \
  --namespace <namespace> \
  --set image.tag=<git-sha> \
  --values ./infra/helm/<service-name>/values-prod.yaml \
  --wait --timeout 5m

Replace <service-name> with the service you are deploying (e.g., payment-service), <namespace> with the Kubernetes namespace (e.g., payments), and <git-sha> with the full 40-character Git commit SHA from the build you want to promote.

For services managed via ArgoCD, you should instead update the image tag in the GitOps repo and let ArgoCD sync. The ArgoCD dashboard is at https://argocd.acme.internal.

Verifying a Deployment

After running the deploy command, verify the rollout completed successfully:

kubectl rollout status deployment/<service-name> -n <namespace>

This will block until all pods are running or the timeout is reached. To see the pods directly:

kubectl get pods -n <namespace> -l app=<service-name>

Check that all pods show Running status and that the READY column matches the expected replica count. To view recent events for the deployment:

kubectl describe deployment <service-name> -n <namespace>

Health Checks

Every service at Acme must expose a /healthz endpoint that returns HTTP 200 when the service is ready. Kubernetes liveness and readiness probes are configured in the Helm chart's values.yaml. The readiness probe is checked every 10 seconds with a failure threshold of 3, meaning a pod is removed from the load balancer after 30 seconds of failed health checks.

To manually invoke the health endpoint inside the cluster:

kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -n <namespace> -- curl -s http://localhost:8080/healthz

Rollbacks

If a deployment introduces a regression, roll back immediately using Helm:

helm rollback <service-name> 0 -n <namespace>

The revision 0 rolls back to the previous Helm release. To list available revisions:

helm history <service-name> -n <namespace>

You can also roll back to a specific revision number:

helm rollback <service-name> <revision> -n <namespace>

After rollback, verify pods are healthy and monitor the service's error rate in Grafana for at least 10 minutes before declaring the incident resolved. File a post-incident review in the #incidents Slack channel and create a Jira ticket to track the root cause investigation.

Production Deploy Checklist

Before merging to main and triggering a production deploy, verify:

  • All tests pass in CI (green checkmark on the PR)
  • Staging deployment has been running for at least 30 minutes without errors
  • No active incidents on the status page
  • Feature flags configured correctly for gradual rollout if applicable
  • On-call engineer is notified in #deployments Slack channel

title: OOMKilled Troubleshooting Guide
body:
OOMKilled Troubleshooting Guide

OOMKilled (Out Of Memory Killed) is one of the most common production issues at Acme. This guide explains how to diagnose and fix pods that are being killed by the Kubernetes OOM killer.

Symptoms

A pod that was OOMKilled will show the following signs:

  • Pod status shows OOMKilled or the container exit code is 137
  • The pod restarts repeatedly (increasing RESTARTS count in kubectl get pods)
  • Alerts fire in PagerDuty for PodCrashLooping or HighMemoryUsage
  • Grafana memory panel shows memory usage hitting the configured limit and then dropping to zero (indicating a restart)

To confirm OOMKilled, run:

kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n <namespace>

Look for OOMKilled in the Last State section and reason OOMKilled in the container status.

Immediate Mitigation

If the service is in a crash loop and traffic is being dropped, the fastest mitigation is to temporarily increase the memory limit in the Helm values and redeploy:

helm upgrade <service-name> ./infra/helm/<service-name> \
  --namespace <namespace> \
  --set resources.limits.memory=2Gi \
  --reuse-values --wait

Use double the current limit as a starting point. This buys time to investigate the root cause without extended downtime.

Diagnosing Memory Usage

To understand what is consuming memory, use the following approaches:

Check current resource usage:

kubectl top pods -n <namespace>

Check configured limits:

kubectl get pod <pod-name> -n <namespace> -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].resources}'

Check memory trends in Grafana: Navigate to the service dashboard and look at the "Memory Usage" panel. Compare the trend over the last 24-48 hours to identify if the growth is sudden (memory leak) or gradual (load increase).

Tuning Memory Limits

Our services use JVM (Java/Kotlin) or Python runtimes. For JVM services, the key parameters are:

  • -Xmx: Maximum heap size. Set this to 70-80% of the container memory limit to leave room for off-heap memory (code cache, metaspace, native memory).
  • -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize: Limits metaspace. Default is unlimited, which can cause OOM. Set to 256m for most services.
  • -XX:+UseContainerSupport: Ensures the JVM respects container limits rather than host memory. This should always be enabled.

For Python services, memory tuning focuses on:

  • Worker count: Reduce --workers in gunicorn/uvicorn if each worker is loading large ML models into memory.
  • Batch sizes: If the service processes data in batches, reduce batch size to lower peak memory usage.
  • Connection pools: Each database connection holds memory. Reduce DB_POOL_SIZE if the service has many replicas.

Identifying Memory Leaks

If memory grows unbounded over time rather than stabilizing, you likely have a leak. Steps to investigate:

  1. Enable heap dumps on OOM by adding -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/tmp/heapdump.hprof to JVM flags
  2. Copy the heap dump out with kubectl cp <pod>:/tmp/heapdump.hprof ./heapdump.hprof -n <namespace>
  3. Analyze with Eclipse Memory Analyzer (MAT) or VisualVM
  4. For Python, use memory_profiler or tracemalloc to trace allocations

Common causes of memory leaks at Acme: unbounded caches, event listener accumulation, large objects held in session state, and connection objects not being properly closed.

Long-term Fixes

After stabilizing the service, create a Jira ticket to track the proper fix. Set realistic resource requests and limits based on observed usage plus a 20% buffer. Document the tuning decision in the service's values.yaml with a comment explaining the rationale.

title: CI/CD Pipeline Overview
body:
CI/CD Pipeline Overview

Acme Engineering uses GitHub Actions for CI and ArgoCD for CD. This guide explains the pipeline stages, how to trigger deployments, and how to skip specific steps when needed.

Pipeline Stages

Every pull request and push to main triggers the following pipeline stages in order:

  1. lint: Runs ESLint/Pylint/ktlint depending on the service language. Fails fast on formatting errors.
  2. test: Runs unit and integration tests. Integration tests spin up Docker Compose services (PostgreSQL, Redis) in the CI environment.
  3. build: Builds the Docker image and tags it with the Git SHA. Pushes to registry.acme.internal/<service>:<sha>.
  4. scan: Runs Trivy vulnerability scan on the built image. High or Critical CVEs cause the pipeline to fail unless explicitly suppressed.
  5. staging-deploy: Automatically updates the image tag in the GitOps repo and ArgoCD syncs the staging cluster. Runs only on pushes to main.
  6. e2e: Runs end-to-end tests against the staging deployment. Uses Playwright for frontend services and k6 for API services.
  7. prod-deploy: Requires manual approval via GitHub environment protection rules. Approved by the on-call engineer or team lead.

Triggering a Deployment

Normal deployments happen automatically when a PR is merged to main. The pipeline runs stages 1-6 automatically. Stage 7 (prod-deploy) requires a human to click "Approve" in the GitHub Actions UI or respond to the Slack notification in #deployments.

To trigger a manual re-run of a failed pipeline:

gh run rerun <run-id> --failed

Or use the GitHub Actions UI to re-run failed jobs.

Skipping Pipeline Steps

To skip specific pipeline stages, add keywords to your commit message:

  • [skip lint]: Skips the lint stage (use only for documentation-only changes)
  • [skip test]: Skips tests (requires team lead approval — do not use in normal circumstances)
  • [skip scan]: Skips vulnerability scanning (requires security team sign-off)
  • [skip ci]: Skips the entire pipeline (use only for README or config-only changes)

Skip flags are logged and audited. Excessive use of [skip test] will be flagged in your quarterly review.

Pipeline Configuration

Pipeline configuration lives in .github/workflows/ in each service repository. The main workflow file is ci.yml. Shared workflow templates are in the acme-org/.github repository and referenced with uses: acme-org/.github/.github/workflows/shared-build.yml@main.

Environment variables and secrets are managed in GitHub repository settings. Service-specific secrets (e.g., API keys for third-party services) must be added to both the staging and production GitHub environments.

Troubleshooting Common Failures

Build fails with "image pull error": Check that the base image tag in the Dockerfile exists. If using latest, pin to a specific digest instead.

Tests fail in CI but pass locally: Usually caused by timezone differences or missing environment variables. Check the CI logs for missing env vars and ensure your docker-compose.test.yml matches the CI environment.

Staging deploy hangs: Check ArgoCD health at https://argocd.acme.internal. Look for sync errors or degraded applications. Also check if the new pods are failing health checks.

E2E tests are flaky: Re-run the pipeline first. If consistently failing, check if a dependent service in staging was recently updated. Post in #platform-eng if the issue persists.

title: API Authentication Setup
body:
API Authentication Setup

Acme's internal and external APIs use JWT (JSON Web Tokens) for authentication. This guide covers how authentication works, how to rotate tokens, and how to debug common 401 errors.

How JWT Authentication Works

All API requests must include an Authorization header with a Bearer token:

Authorization: Bearer <jwt-token>

The API gateway validates the token on every request. The JWT contains claims including sub (subject/user ID), iss (issuer), aud (audience), exp (expiry timestamp), and custom claims like org_id and role.

Tokens are signed using RS256 (RSA SHA-256). The public keys are published at https://auth.acme.internal/.well-known/jwks.json and each service validates tokens against this JWKS endpoint. Public keys are cached for 15 minutes to reduce latency.

Token Rotation Procedure

API tokens for service-to-service communication are stored in Vault at secret/services/<service-name>/api-token. To rotate a token:

  1. Generate a new token via the admin API: POST /admin/api/tokens with the service account credentials
  2. Write the new token to Vault: vault kv put secret/services/<service-name>/api-token value=<new-token>
  3. Trigger a rolling restart of the consuming service to pick up the new secret: kubectl rollout restart deployment/<service-name> -n <namespace>
  4. Verify the service is healthy after the restart
  5. Revoke the old token via the admin API: DELETE /admin/api/tokens/<old-token-id>

Important: Steps 3 and 5 must not be reversed. Revoke the old token only after confirming the service is using the new one. Revoking first causes a window where the service has no valid token and returns 401 errors to its clients.

Common Causes of 401 Errors

If you are seeing 401 Unauthorized responses, work through this checklist:

  • Token expired: Check the exp claim in the JWT (decode at jwt.io). Short-lived tokens (15 minute default for user tokens) need refresh. Service tokens have 90-day expiry.
  • Wrong audience: The aud claim must match the API's expected audience. Cross-service calls must use a token scoped for the target service. Check the API's documentation for its expected audience value.
  • Token not yet valid: The nbf (not before) claim causes rejections if the issuing server's clock is ahead of the validating server's clock by more than the allowed skew (5 seconds at Acme).
  • Public key cache stale after rotation: After rotating the signing key, services cache the old public key for up to 15 minutes. During this window, tokens signed with the new key will fail validation. Wait 15 minutes or force a restart of the affected service.
  • Token revoked: If a token was explicitly revoked, it will return 401 even if not expired. Check the revocation list in the auth service admin panel.
  • Missing or malformed header: Ensure the header is exactly Authorization: Bearer <token> with no extra spaces or different casing in "Bearer".

Debugging 401 Errors

To debug interactively, decode the JWT and inspect claims:

echo <token> | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | python3 -m json.tool

Check the auth service logs for the specific rejection reason:

kubectl logs -n auth -l app=auth-service --since=5m | grep "401\|unauthorized\|invalid token"

The auth service logs include the specific reason for rejection (e.g., "token expired", "invalid signature", "audience mismatch").

Token Expiry Configuration

Token lifetimes are configured in the auth service's config.yaml:

  • User session tokens: 15 minutes (with refresh token valid for 7 days)
  • Service-to-service tokens: 90 days
  • CI/CD tokens: 1 hour
  • Admin tokens: 1 hour with mandatory MFA

To change token lifetimes, submit a PR to the auth-service repository modifying config/token-policy.yaml. Changes require review from the security team.

title: Database Connection Guide
body:
Database Connection Guide

Acme's primary database is PostgreSQL 15, hosted on AWS RDS. This guide covers connection strings, connection pool configuration, and how to diagnose and fix connection-related errors.

Connection Strings

Database connection strings follow this format:

postgresql://<user>:<password>@<host>:5432/<database>?sslmode=require

For production, the connection string is injected via the environment variable DATABASE_URL, populated from Vault. Never hardcode credentials in source code.

Read replicas are available for read-heavy workloads at DATABASE_URL_REPLICA. Use the replica for analytics queries, reports, and any read operation that can tolerate up to 30 seconds of replication lag.

Connection strings by environment:

  • Production: injected from Vault at secret/prod/<service>/database
  • Staging: injected from Vault at secret/staging/<service>/database
  • Local development: use postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/<service>_dev

Connection Pool Configuration

All services must use a connection pool rather than creating new connections per request. The recommended settings vary by service type:

Web/API services (many replicas, short-lived requests):

  • pool_size: 5 per pod replica
  • max_overflow: 10
  • pool_timeout: 30 seconds
  • pool_recycle: 1800 seconds (recycle connections every 30 min to avoid stale connections)

Worker services (few replicas, long-running jobs):

  • pool_size: 2 per pod replica
  • max_overflow: 5
  • pool_recycle: 3600 seconds

PostgreSQL has a maximum connection limit of 500 on our RDS instance. With ~50 pod replicas across all services, a pool size of 5 per pod = 250 max connections, well within the limit with headroom for admin connections and replicas.

Connection Pool Exhaustion

Symptoms of connection pool exhaustion:

  • Errors like QueuePool limit of size 5 overflow 10 reached (SQLAlchemy) or too many connections (psycopg2)
  • Request latency spikes as requests queue waiting for a connection
  • Eventual timeout errors (504 Gateway Timeout)

Immediate fix: Identify which service is holding too many connections:

SELECT count(*), application_name 
FROM pg_stat_activity 
WHERE state != 'idle' 
GROUP BY application_name 
ORDER BY count DESC;

If one service dominates, rolling restart it to close leaked connections:

kubectl rollout restart deployment/<service-name> -n <namespace>

Timeout Configuration

Configure statement and connection timeouts to prevent runaway queries from blocking the pool:

  • Statement timeout: 30 seconds for API services (options=-c statement_timeout=30000 in the connection string)
  • Idle-in-transaction timeout: 60 seconds (idle_in_transaction_session_timeout=60000)
  • Connect timeout: 5 seconds (driver-level, prevents slow connects from blocking threads)

Common Errors and Fixes

SSL SYSCALL error: EOF detected: The database server closed the connection. Usually caused by RDS maintenance or instance restart. Pool recycle should handle this automatically. If persistent, increase pool_pre_ping=True to test connections before using them.

could not connect to server: Connection refused: Check that the database host is correct and the security group allows connections from the service's pod CIDR. Verify with nc -zv <db-host> 5432 from inside the pod.

password authentication failed: Credentials have changed or the Vault lease expired. Re-read from Vault and trigger a pod restart.

title: Incident Response Playbook
body:
Incident Response Playbook

This playbook defines how Acme Engineering responds to production incidents. Following this process ensures we resolve incidents quickly and minimize customer impact.

Severity Levels

Incidents are classified into four severity levels:

  • SEV-1 (Critical): Complete service outage or data loss. Affects all or most customers. Requires immediate response 24/7. SLA: acknowledge within 5 minutes, mitigate within 30 minutes.
  • SEV-2 (High): Significant degradation affecting many customers (e.g., >10% error rate, >5x latency). SLA: acknowledge within 15 minutes, mitigate within 2 hours.
  • SEV-3 (Medium): Limited impact, workaround exists. Affects a small subset of customers or non-critical features. SLA: acknowledge within 1 hour, resolve within 24 hours.
  • SEV-4 (Low): Cosmetic issues or minor degradation with no customer impact. Treated as a Jira bug with normal priority.

PagerDuty Escalation

Alerts from Grafana/Prometheus automatically create PagerDuty incidents for SEV-1 and SEV-2 conditions. The on-call engineer is paged via the schedule at https://acme.pagerduty.com/schedules.

If the primary on-call does not acknowledge within 10 minutes, PagerDuty automatically escalates to the secondary on-call. If unacknowledged for 20 minutes, the engineering manager is paged.

To manually trigger a PagerDuty incident: post !incident create SEV-2 Brief description here in the #incidents Slack channel. The PagerDuty bot will create the incident and notify the on-call engineer.

Incident Response Steps

  1. Acknowledge: Acknowledge the PagerDuty alert within the SLA. Post in #incidents Slack channel: "I'm on it. Investigating [service name] issue."
  2. Assess: Determine the blast radius. How many customers are affected? What is the error rate? Check Grafana dashboards for the affected service.
  3. Mitigate: Apply the fastest available mitigation. This may be a rollback, a feature flag toggle, or scaling up replicas. Speed matters more than elegance during mitigation.
  4. Communicate: Post a status update in #incidents every 15 minutes until resolved. Update the external status page at https://status.acme.com for customer-facing incidents.
  5. Resolve: Confirm the issue is fixed by checking error rates return to baseline. Close the PagerDuty incident. Post a final update in #incidents.
  6. Post-Incident Review: Within 48 hours for SEV-1/SEV-2, create a post-incident review document. Template is in Confluence at ENG-PIR-TEMPLATE. Share in #incidents and schedule a 30-minute review meeting.

Common Mitigation Actions

Quick reference for common mitigations:

  • Rollback a bad deployment: helm rollback <service> 0 -n <namespace>
  • Scale up replicas: kubectl scale deployment/<service> --replicas=<n> -n <namespace>
  • Toggle feature flag: Use the LaunchDarkly dashboard at https://app.launchdarkly.com
  • Force pod restart: kubectl rollout restart deployment/<service> -n <namespace>
  • Check recent deploys: helm history <service> -n <namespace>

Escalation Contacts

If you need additional help during an incident:

  • Platform/Infrastructure issues: #platform-eng Slack channel, or page the platform on-call
  • Database issues: Ping the DBA on-call via PagerDuty escalation policy "Database"
  • Security incidents: Immediately contact the security team via #security-incidents (private channel)
  • Vendor/AWS issues: Check the AWS Health Dashboard and contact AWS support if needed (P1 support plan active)

title: New Engineer Onboarding
body:
New Engineer Onboarding

Welcome to Acme Engineering! This guide will help you get set up in your first week. By the end of day 5, you should have access to all the tools you need and have submitted your first PR.

Access Setup

On your first day, your manager should have submitted an IT ticket to provision your accounts. Verify you have access to the following systems and ping your manager if anything is missing:

  • GitHub: You should be a member of the acme-org GitHub organization. Verify at https://github.com/orgs/acme-org/members.
  • AWS Console: Log in via SSO at https://acme.awsapps.com/start with your Okta credentials. You should have read access to the acme-dev AWS account by default.
  • Kubernetes: Request kubeconfig by posting in #platform-eng: "Hi, I'm new and need kubeconfig for staging." The platform team will set up your RBAC and send you instructions.
  • Vault: Access is granted when you need secrets for a specific service. Request from your team lead.
  • PagerDuty: You will be added to the on-call rotation after 90 days. Your manager will add you to the schedule when it's time.
  • Staging environment: Access to staging apps is via VPN. Install the VPN client (Tailscale) using the instructions in the IT wiki at https://it.acme.internal/vpn-setup.

First PR

Your goal for week 1 is to submit a PR, no matter how small. Good starting points:

  • Fix a typo in documentation
  • Add a missing unit test
  • Resolve a good-first-issue labeled Jira ticket

The PR process at Acme:

  1. Create a branch: git checkout -b your-name/description-of-change
  2. Make your change and commit: git commit -m "ENG-XXX: Brief description" (include the Jira ticket number)
  3. Push and open a PR on GitHub: gh pr create --fill
  4. At least 1 approval required from a team member. 2 approvals for changes to core services.
  5. CI must pass before merging. Merge by squashing commits using the "Squash and merge" button.

Essential Tools

Install the following tools on your laptop:

  • kubectl: brew install kubectl
  • helm: brew install helm
  • gh (GitHub CLI): brew install gh
  • terraform: brew install terraform
  • Docker Desktop: Download from https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop
  • VS Code with recommended extensions: listed in .vscode/extensions.json in each repo

Key Slack Channels

Join these channels in Slack:

  • #engineering: General engineering announcements
  • #deployments: Deployment notifications (automated + manual)
  • #incidents: Production incidents
  • #platform-eng: Platform team support and announcements
  • #your-team-name: Your team's channel (ask your manager)

title: Service Architecture Overview
body:
Service Architecture Overview

Acme Engineering runs a microservices architecture on Kubernetes. This document describes the major services, their dependencies, and the ports they expose.

Core Services

The following services form the core of the Acme platform:

  • api-gateway (port 8080): Entry point for all external traffic. Handles routing, rate limiting, JWT validation, and TLS termination. Runs in the gateway namespace.
  • user-service (port 8081): Manages user accounts, authentication, and session management. Depends on PostgreSQL (primary) and Redis (session cache). Namespace: users.
  • payment-service (port 8082): Processes payments via Stripe. Has elevated security controls and runs in an isolated namespace payments with strict network policies.
  • order-service (port 8083): Manages order lifecycle (creation, fulfillment, cancellation). Depends on payment-service, inventory-service, and notification-service.
  • inventory-service (port 8084): Tracks product inventory. Exposes a gRPC API on port 9084 for internal service-to-service calls.
  • notification-service (port 8085): Sends emails, SMS, and push notifications. Uses AWS SES for email and Twilio for SMS.
  • recommendation-service (port 8086): ML-powered product recommendations. Memory-intensive due to model loading. Runs on nodes with extended memory (16GB+ RAM nodes).
  • data-pipeline (batch): Nightly ETL job that processes analytics data. Runs as a Kubernetes CronJob at 02:00 UTC.

Service Dependencies Map

External request flow:

Client → api-gateway → user-service (auth check)
                     → order-service → payment-service → Stripe
                                     → inventory-service
                                     → notification-service → AWS SES / Twilio
       api-gateway → recommendation-service (product pages)

Infrastructure Components

Shared infrastructure components:

  • PostgreSQL (AWS RDS, port 5432): Primary datastore for user-service, payment-service, and order-service. Each service has its own database schema.
  • Redis (AWS ElastiCache, port 6379): Used by user-service (session cache), api-gateway (rate limit counters), and recommendation-service (recommendation cache).
  • Kafka (MSK, port 9092): Event bus. order-service publishes order events; notification-service and data-pipeline consume them.
  • S3: Static assets, ML model artifacts, and data pipeline outputs.

Network Policies

Default-deny network policies are in place. Only explicitly allowed service-to-service communication is permitted. The network policy definitions are in infra/k8s/network-policies/. If you add a new service dependency, you must add a corresponding network policy allowing the traffic.

Service Discovery

Services discover each other via Kubernetes DNS. The format is <service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local. For example, order-service calls payment-service at http://payment-service.payments.svc.cluster.local:8082.

title: Rate Limiting Configuration
body:
Rate Limiting Configuration

Acme's API gateway enforces rate limits to protect backend services from overload and abuse. This guide explains the rate limit tiers, how to configure them, and how to handle 429 responses.

Rate Limit Tiers

Rate limits are applied per API key and vary by customer tier:

  • Free tier: 60 requests per minute, 1,000 requests per day
  • Starter tier: 300 requests per minute, 10,000 requests per day
  • Professional tier: 1,000 requests per minute, 100,000 requests per day
  • Enterprise tier: 5,000 requests per minute, unlimited per day (configurable per contract)

Limits are tracked using a sliding window algorithm. The counters are stored in Redis (rate-limit:<api-key>:<window-start>) and reset on a rolling 60-second window.

Rate Limit Response Headers

Every API response includes rate limit headers:

  • X-RateLimit-Limit: The maximum number of requests allowed in the current window
  • X-RateLimit-Remaining: The number of requests remaining in the current window
  • X-RateLimit-Reset: Unix timestamp when the window resets
  • Retry-After: Number of seconds to wait before retrying (only present in 429 responses)

Handling 429 Too Many Requests

When rate limited, clients should:

  1. Read the Retry-After header to know exactly how long to wait
  2. Implement exponential backoff with jitter rather than hammering the endpoint at fixed intervals
  3. Cache responses where possible to reduce request volume
  4. Batch API calls where the API supports it (e.g., bulk endpoints)

Example backoff strategy:

delay = min(initial_delay * 2^attempt + random_jitter, max_delay)

Configuring Enterprise Tier Limits

Enterprise tier limits are configured per-customer in the api-gateway configuration file at infra/k8s/api-gateway/rate-limits.yaml. Each entry specifies the API key prefix and the custom limit:

enterprise_overrides:
  - key_prefix: "ent_acmecorp_"
    rpm: 10000
    rpd: null  # unlimited
  - key_prefix: "ent_bigcorp_"
    rpm: 5000
    rpd: 500000

After editing the file, apply via GitOps (commit and push) or for urgent changes: kubectl apply -f infra/k8s/api-gateway/rate-limits.yaml

Monitoring Rate Limit Usage

Rate limit metrics are available in Grafana on the "API Gateway" dashboard. Key panels:

  • "Rate Limited Requests" panel shows which API keys are hitting limits most frequently
  • "Top Consumers" shows the highest-volume API keys over the selected time range
  • Use this data to proactively reach out to customers approaching their limits before they experience throttling

title: Monitoring and Alerting Guide
body:
Monitoring and Alerting Guide

Acme uses Prometheus for metrics collection, Grafana for visualization, and PagerDuty for on-call alerting. This guide explains how to use these tools, what the alert thresholds are, and how to manage alert noise.

Grafana Dashboards

Access Grafana at https://grafana.acme.internal. Login with your Okta SSO credentials.

Key dashboards for daily operations:

  • Platform Overview: High-level health of all services. Start here during incidents.
  • Service Dashboards: One dashboard per microservice showing request rate, error rate, latency (p50/p95/p99), and resource usage. Find them under the "Services" folder.
  • Kubernetes: Pod health, node resource usage, OOM events, and pod restarts. Use the "Kubernetes / Compute Resources / Pod" dashboard for per-pod details.
  • Database: RDS metrics including active connections, query latency, and replication lag.

Alert Thresholds

Standard alert thresholds (adjustable per service in infra/monitoring/alerts/):

  • Error rate > 5% for 5 minutes → SEV-2 PagerDuty alert
  • p99 latency > 2 seconds for 10 minutes → SEV-2 PagerDuty alert
  • Pod restart count > 3 in 15 minutes → SEV-2 PagerDuty alert
  • Memory usage > 85% of limit → Warning Slack notification to #alerts
  • Memory usage > 95% of limit → SEV-2 PagerDuty alert
  • CPU throttling > 50% for 10 minutes → Warning Slack notification
  • Database connections > 400 (of 500 max) → SEV-2 PagerDuty alert

Silencing Alerts

During planned maintenance or after a known issue is identified but a fix is in progress, you can silence alerts to reduce noise:

Method 1 — Grafana Silence (preferred): In Grafana, navigate to Alerting → Silences. Click "New Silence". Fill in the label matchers (e.g., service=payment-service), set the duration, and add a comment explaining why the alert is silenced. Silences are visible to the whole team.

Method 2 — PagerDuty Maintenance Window: For planned maintenance windows longer than 2 hours, create a maintenance window in PagerDuty. This suppresses all alerts for the selected services during the window.

Important: Do not silence alerts indefinitely. Maximum recommended silence duration is 4 hours. If a fix will take longer, keep the alert active so it serves as a reminder.

Viewing Logs

Application logs are aggregated in Loki and searchable through Grafana's Explore view.

To view logs for a specific pod directly via kubectl:

kubectl logs <pod-name> -n <namespace>

To follow logs in real-time:

kubectl logs -f <pod-name> -n <namespace>

To view logs from the previous container instance (useful after a crash):

kubectl logs <pod-name> -n <namespace> --previous

To filter logs by time (last 30 minutes):

kubectl logs <pod-name> -n <namespace> --since=30m

For logs across all pods of a deployment, use the label selector:

kubectl logs -n <namespace> -l app=<service-name> --since=1h

Adding New Alerts

Alert rules are defined in YAML files in infra/monitoring/alerts/<service-name>.yaml. The format follows the Prometheus alerting rule spec. Submit a PR to add or modify alert thresholds. New alert rules require review from the platform team to ensure they follow naming conventions and have appropriate severity labels.

title: Setting Up a New CI/CD Pipeline
body:
Setting Up a New CI/CD Pipeline

This guide walks you through creating a CI/CD pipeline for a new service at Acme from scratch using GitHub Actions and ArgoCD.

Prerequisites

Before setting up a pipeline you need: a GitHub repository for your service, access to the acme-org/.github shared workflows repository, a Kubernetes namespace provisioned by the platform team, and a Docker registry path reserved at registry.acme.internal/<team>/<service>. File a ticket in the PLAT Jira project to request namespace and registry provisioning if you don't already have them.

Creating the Workflow File

Create .github/workflows/ci.yml in your repository. Start from the shared template:

uses: acme-org/.github/.github/workflows/service-ci.yml@main
with:
  service-name: my-service
  registry: registry.acme.internal/myteam/my-service
  namespace: my-namespace
  helm-chart-path: ./infra/helm/my-service
secrets: inherit

The shared template handles lint, test, build, scan, and staging deploy stages automatically. You only need to override values specific to your service.

Configuring Environments

GitHub environments gate production deployments. Create two environments in your repo settings: staging (no protection rules, deploys automatically on merge to main) and production (required reviewers: on-call engineer or your team lead). Add environment-specific secrets under each environment — never use repository-level secrets for environment-specific values.

Setting Up ArgoCD

ArgoCD manages the GitOps sync from the acme-gitops repository. To register your new service, add an Application manifest to acme-gitops/apps/<team>/<service>.yaml:

apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: my-service
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/acme-org/acme-gitops
    targetRevision: HEAD
    path: services/my-service
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: my-namespace
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: true
      selfHeal: true

After merging this manifest, ArgoCD will begin syncing your service automatically. Verify at https://argocd.acme.internal.

First Deployment

Push to main and watch the pipeline run in GitHub Actions. The first run typically takes 8-12 minutes. Staging deploy happens automatically; production deploy requires approval. Check the #deployments Slack channel for notifications. If the pipeline fails, check the individual step logs — build failures are usually missing base image tags, test failures are usually missing environment variables in the CI environment.

title: GitHub Actions Advanced Configuration
body:
GitHub Actions Advanced Configuration

This page covers advanced GitHub Actions patterns used at Acme for caching, matrix builds, conditional jobs, and self-hosted runners.

Caching Dependencies

Always cache dependencies to keep pipeline times under 5 minutes. Use the actions/cache action with keys based on dependency lock files:

- uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: ~/.npm
    key: ${{ runner.os }}-npm-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
    restore-keys: |
      ${{ runner.os }}-npm-

For Python services, cache the pip wheel directory. For JVM services, cache the Gradle or Maven local repository. A well-configured cache reduces install time from 3-4 minutes to under 30 seconds.

Matrix Builds

For libraries or services that need to be tested across multiple versions, use a matrix strategy:

strategy:
  matrix:
    node-version: [18, 20, 22]
    os: [ubuntu-latest]

Matrix builds run in parallel. Use fail-fast: false if you want all matrix variants to complete even when one fails, which is useful for spotting version-specific failures.

Conditional Jobs

Use if conditions to skip expensive jobs when not needed. Common patterns at Acme:

# Only run e2e tests on main branch
if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'

# Skip if PR is labeled 'skip-e2e'
if: "!contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'skip-e2e')"

# Only run for changes in specific paths
if: github.event_name == 'push' || contains(github.event.pull_request.changed_files, 'src/')

Self-Hosted Runners

Acme maintains self-hosted runners for jobs that require internal network access (integration tests against staging databases, vulnerability scans against internal registries). To use a self-hosted runner, set runs-on: [self-hosted, linux, x64]. Self-hosted runners are in the acme-runners pool and are managed by the platform team. File a PLAT ticket to request additional runner capacity.

Secrets Management

Never log secrets. Use ${{ secrets.MY_SECRET }} syntax — GitHub automatically masks these values in logs. For dynamic secrets (tokens generated during a job), use ::add-mask::$VALUE to mask them manually. Rotate secrets quarterly; the platform team sends reminders via the #security Slack channel.

title: Deployment Rollback Runbook
body:
Deployment Rollback Runbook

This runbook describes how to roll back a bad deployment quickly and safely. Speed matters — a bad deployment rolling back in under 5 minutes is the target.

When to Roll Back

Roll back immediately if: error rate increases by more than 5% over baseline, p99 latency exceeds 2x the 7-day average, any critical service is returning 5xx for more than 60 seconds, or on-call receives a PagerDuty alert within 10 minutes of a deployment completing.

Do not wait to investigate root cause before rolling back during an active incident. Roll back first, investigate after service is restored.

Helm Rollback

The fastest path is a Helm rollback. Run from your local machine with production kubeconfig:

# List available revisions
helm history <service-name> -n <namespace>

# Roll back to the previous revision
helm rollback <service-name> 0 -n <namespace>

# Roll back to a specific revision
helm rollback <service-name> <revision-number> -n <namespace>

Helm rollback is instantaneous — it submits the change and Kubernetes performs a rolling update. Monitor rollout status:

kubectl rollout status deployment/<service-name> -n <namespace> --timeout=5m

ArgoCD Rollback

If the service is managed by ArgoCD, use the ArgoCD UI or CLI to roll back to a previous sync revision. In the ArgoCD UI, navigate to the application, click History and Rollback, and select the last known good sync. ArgoCD will deploy the previous GitOps state.

argocd app rollback <app-name> <history-id>

GitOps Manual Revert

If Helm and ArgoCD rollbacks are not available, revert the image tag in the GitOps repository:

  1. Find the last known good Git SHA from the GitHub Actions run history
  2. Update the image tag in acme-gitops/services/<service>/values.yaml
  3. Merge the revert PR immediately (use the emergency bypass label if needed)
  4. ArgoCD will auto-sync within 3 minutes

Post-Rollback

After confirming service is restored: post in #incidents with a brief summary, update the incident ticket status to "Mitigated", and schedule a post-incident review within 48 hours. Identify the root cause and create a follow-up Jira ticket before closing the incident.
